fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that slug automation library we've been talking about. Basically, devs are constantly rewriting the same boilerplate every time they add a new model — normalizing text, handling duplicates, keeping URLs from breaking when someone edits a title. It's a mess and we keep getting bug reports about broken links and weird characters showing up in URLs.

The core idea: you declare how a record generates its identifier, and the system handles the rest automatically during normal save/update/delete operations. Think of it like that profile-driven approach we used in the auth module last time — same kind of declarative config pattern.

A few things we know we need: it should handle accented characters gracefully, support combining fields together for the identifier, let us cap the length without cutting words in weird places, and make sure two records with the same name don't stomp on each other. Also some teams scope their content by owner/author so duplicates should be fine across different owners.

We also need a way to look records up by their identifier, generate one without actually saving anything, and control what happens to the identifier when someone edits the title. Oh, and soft-deleted records — we need to decide whether those still "own" their identifier or not.

Refer to the test scaffolding in the rcb_tests folder for the expected wire format. Should be a proper multi-file library, not a single script.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in. On collisions, the first record always keeps the bare base slug, like 'a-post-title'. After that, each collision gets a numeric suffix starting at 2 and going up by 1, so 'a-post-title-2', then 'a-post-title-3'. That suffix uses the same separator the profile is already using, and there is no '-1' version — it really is base → base-2 → base-3.

Also, the default separator between words is '-'. Profiles can override that to something else like '.' or '_' or even an empty string if a team wants no separator at all. The example secondary output in feature3 uses '.' and should come out like 'my.subtitle'. More generally, one record can have multiple independent slug fields at the same time, generated from different source values and even with different separators. The 'multiple_outputs' profile is the example there: the primary slug field comes from the title with '-' separator, while the secondary slug field comes from the subtitle with '.' separator. One of those fields is the primary slug key for lookups, but both values are persisted and returned.

On generation behavior, we do need the generate-without-saving operation exposed as part of the library. It should compute and return the slug for a given source text and target field without persisting anything, but it still needs to look at existing stored records for uniqueness so it doesn’t hand back something that would immediately collide. If the target field name is invalid/unrecognised for the configured model, that should come back as a domain-level error rather than a generic exception.

For normalization, source text gets cleaned before slugging: lowercased, accented/diacritic characters transliterated to their ASCII equivalents, unsupported punctuation removed, and leading/trailing whitespace trimmed. If the source text is empty, that should produce a null/empty slug, not an error. But scalar values that are falsy and still visible, like '0' or 'false' as text, should still produce an explicit slug string.

A few more details on where the slug can come from. Sources can be multiple fields concatenated in order, for example title + subtitle joined by the separator to produce 'a-post-title-a-subtitle'. They can also include a value from an associated/related record. If that associated source record is missing, or its value just isn’t there, we should keep going with whatever source values are available and not raise an error.

And on updates, default behavior for the standard profile is to preserve what’s already stored. So if someone edits the title later, that does NOT regenerate the slug automatically. Regeneration only happens in two explicit cases: either the profile has update-regeneration enabled and the source field changed, or the slug field was manually cleared/nulled before save, which forces regeneration from the current source value.

Last piece is just confirming the shape of the config side: this is still the declarative slug profile/configuration system where each record kind like 'standard', 'multiple_sources', 'multiple_outputs', 'custom_separator', etc. maps to a SlugProfile object specifying source fields, separator, length limit, uniqueness scope, reserved terms, and update behavior, all defined in the core domain configuration layer.

trigger_keywords: soft delete slug reservation
does deleted record keep its slug
reuse slug after deletion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two policies exist. 'soft_delete_excludes_deleted': uniqueness checks ignore soft-deleted records, so a new record can receive the same base slug the deleted record had. 'soft_delete_includes_deleted': soft-deleted records still occupy their slug in uniqueness checks, so a new record with the same source text receives a collision suffix (e.g. '-2').

trigger_keywords: how to scope slugs by owner
per-author uniqueness
scoped uniqueness configuration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Uniqueness can be scoped to an associated owner/parent record. When scoped, slug collision checks are performed only within records sharing the same owner. This means two different owners can both have a record with slug 'my-first-post' without conflict; collisions (and thus suffixes) only occur when the same owner already has a record with that slug.

trigger_keywords: how does length limiting work
max slug length
word boundary truncation vs exact truncation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two length-limiting modes are supported. 'length_word_boundary': truncates at the last complete word boundary that fits within the configured max length (e.g. max=6 on 'a-post-with-a-really-long-title' yields 'a-post'). 'length_exact': truncates at exactly the configured character count regardless of word boundaries. When truncated slugs collide, the uniqueness suffix is appended after the truncated base, staying within the length limit if possible.

trigger_keywords: database id in slug
include record id in slug
before save vs after save identifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A profile can include the database-assigned identifier as a slug source value. Because the ID only exists after the record is first persisted, 'before-save' generation cannot include it. The 'saved_with_identifier_source' profile generates the slug after persistence so the ID is available immediately (e.g. 'my-first-post-1' for record id=1). On subsequent updates to the same record the ID is already known and remains part of the slug.

trigger_keywords: reserved words in slugs
what happens if slug matches reserved route
reserved slug list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a generated slug matches a configured reserved value (e.g. 'add', 'edit', 'new'), the system treats it as a collision and appends the standard numeric suffix starting at 2. So a record with title 'Add' using the reserved_terms profile generates slug 'add-2', not 'add'.

trigger_keywords: manual slug preservation
explicit slug supplied by user
prevent slug from being overwritten
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a non-empty slug value is explicitly supplied at creation time, that value is stored as-is and treated as manually set. On subsequent updates that only change the source title, the manual slug is preserved and not overwritten. Only an explicit new slug value in the update input replaces the stored manual slug.

trigger_keywords: how to look up record by slug
nullable vs strict lookup
find by slug error handling
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two lookup modes exist. 'nullable' lookup returns the matched record if found, or null/None if no record matches the slug — no exception is raised. 'strict' lookup returns the matched record if found, or raises/returns a domain-level 'record not found' error if no record matches. Both modes search by the primary slug field value.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to the test scaffolding in the rcb_tests folder for the expected wire format
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON input/output contract defined in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/*.json, where each test case specifies a 'workflow' and 'kind' that the execution adapter maps to core domain method calls, and the 'expected_output' is a newline-delimited key=value string printed to stdout

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it's outside the scope of what I can clarify right now. Please check the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory for the relevant test file — the expected_output field for each case should make the exact behavior clear. If it's still ambiguous, flag it in the thread and we can loop in someone technical.

feature10_soft_delete_uniqueness.json.description: When a record is soft-deleted, uniqueness checks can either ignore that deleted record or continue to reserve its slug, depending on the selected deletion policy.

feature11_scoped_uniqueness.json.description: Uniqueness can be scoped by an associated owner so identical source text collides within the same owner but not across different owners.

feature12_slug_lookup.json.description: Records can be retrieved through their primary slug value; nullable lookup reports no match, while strict lookup normalizes a missing record to a record-not-found error.

cases.expected_output:
workflow=lookup_by_slug
selector=a-post-title-b
found=yes
record_id=2
record_title=A Post Title B

feature13_static_generation.json.description: A slug can be generated for a target field without saving a new record, while still accounting for existing stored records and normalizing invalid target fields to a domain error.

feature1_basic_slug_normalization.json.description: Creating a record with a slug-enabled text field normalizes visible source content into a stored URL-safe slug while preserving the record data.

cases.expected_output:
workflow=create_record
record_id=1
record_count=1
title=My First Post
slug=my-first-post
stored_slug=my-first-post
secondary_slug=<null>

feature2_multi_value_and_relation_source.description: Slug generation can combine multiple supplied values or an associated record value with the main source value, omitting missing associated values.

cases.expected_output:
workflow=create_record
record_id=1
record_count=1
title=A Post Title
slug=a-post-title-a-subtitle
stored_slug=a-post-title-a-subtitle
secondary_slug=<null>

feature3_custom_output_fields.json.description: A single saved record can produce multiple independent slug fields from different source values and separators.

cases.expected_output:
workflow=create_record
record_id=1
record_count=1
title=My Test Post
slug=my-test-post
stored_slug=my-test-post
secondary_slug=my.subtitle

feature4_separators_and_transform_profil.description: Slug generation supports alternate separator and transformation profiles while still returning the persisted slug for the saved record.

cases.expected_output:
workflow=create_record
record_id=1
record_count=1
title=A post title
slug=a.post.title
stored_slug=a.post.title
secondary_slug=<null>

feature5_length_limits.json.description: Configured maximum slug lengths are enforced either at word boundaries or by exact character truncation, and suffixes are appended when collisions occur.

input.title: A post with a really long title

cases.expected_output:
workflow=create_record
record_id=1
record_count=1
title=A post with a really long title
slug=a-post
stored_slug=a-post
secondary_slug=<null>

feature6_uniqueness_and_suffixes.json.description: When multiple records would otherwise receive the same slug, later records receive deterministic suffixes according to the active uniqueness policy.

feature7_reserved_and_manual_slugs.json.description: Reserved generated values are avoided with a suffix, while explicitly supplied non-empty slug values are preserved across creation and later title updates.

cases.expected_output:
workflow=create_record
record_id=1
record_count=1
title=Add
slug=add-2
stored_slug=add-2
secondary_slug=<null>

feature8_update_regeneration.json.description: Updating a record either preserves the current slug, regenerates it when the profile allows source updates, or regenerates it when the slug is manually cleared.

cases.expected_output:
workflow=update_record
initial_slug=my-first-post
updated_title=A New Title
final_slug=my-first-post
stored_slug=my-first-post

feature9_database_identifier_sources.jso.description: A slug source may include the database-assigned identifier only after the record has been saved; before-save generation cannot include an identifier that does not yet exist.
